title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice on a health and fitness website:

'We are looking for reviews of local gyms and fitness centres! Tell us about the facilities, the staff, the atmosphere, and whether you would recommend it to others.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
PeakFit Gym — Is It Worth Joining?

Having been a member of PeakFit Gym for six months, I feel well-placed to offer an honest assessment of what it has to offer.

In terms of facilities, the gym is genuinely impressive. There is a wide range of modern cardio and weights equipment, a dedicated stretching area, and two large studios used for classes including yoga, spin, and high-intensity interval training. Everything is kept exceptionally clean, which I greatly appreciate.

The staff deserve particular praise. The personal trainers are knowledgeable and approachable, and new members are offered a free introductory session, which I found extremely useful when I first joined.

The atmosphere is welcoming rather than intimidating, which matters enormously for those who are new to exercise. Even during peak hours, the gym rarely feels uncomfortably crowded.

On the downside, the monthly membership fee is slightly above average for the area, and parking can occasionally be difficult.

Overall, PeakFit Gym offers excellent value for those serious about improving their fitness. I would recommend it without hesitation.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read an article in a newspaper suggesting that physical education lessons should be reduced in schools to allow more time for academic subjects. Write a letter to the newspaper editor expressing your opinion on the importance of PE in schools, giving reasons and examples to support your views.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to the article suggesting that physical education should be reduced in secondary schools. I strongly disagree with this proposal and believe it would be deeply detrimental to young people's wellbeing.

Regular physical activity has well-documented benefits for both physical and mental health. Research consistently demonstrates that students who exercise regularly perform better academically, show improved concentration, and experience lower levels of anxiety and depression. Reducing PE lessons would therefore be counterproductive, even from a purely academic perspective.

Furthermore, schools play a crucial role in instilling healthy habits. Many young people have limited access to sport outside school, meaning that PE lessons represent their only opportunity to develop fitness and learn team skills. Removing this provision would disproportionately affect disadvantaged students.

Instead of cutting PE, I would urge schools to integrate physical activity more creatively into the timetable — perhaps through active learning approaches or lunchtime clubs.

I hope that decision-makers will consider the long-term consequences before making any such changes.

Yours faithfully,
A Concerned Reader

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your school has asked you to write a report on students' physical activity habits, based on a survey you carried out. Include information about how active students currently are, the main barriers they face, and recommendations for encouraging a more active lifestyle among students.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Students' Physical Activity Habits at Westbridge School

Introduction
This report summarises findings from a survey of 150 students aged 14–18, conducted to assess their current levels of physical activity and identify barriers to regular exercise.

Current Activity Levels
The results revealed a concerning picture. Only 28% of respondents met the recommended guideline of 60 minutes of moderate exercise per day. The majority reported spending most of their leisure time on screens, with homework and social commitments cited as reasons for limited physical activity.

Main Barriers
The most commonly reported barriers were lack of time, limited access to affordable sports facilities, and a perceived lack of ability or confidence. A significant number of students also reported feeling excluded from competitive sports due to their skill level.

Recommendations
The school should consider extending lunchtime recreational activities to include non-competitive options such as walking clubs or dance sessions. Subsidised gym passes and an expanded range of after-school clubs would also help engage less active students. Regular fitness challenges with modest rewards could further incentivise participation across all year groups.
